examples.explanation: Cycles: [5,10]. Max cycle = 10. Start time=10: Check 10%5=0 ✓, 10%10=0 ✓. All divisible immediately, return 10.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Iteration
approaches.summary: Iterate through time steps starting from the maximum task cycle, checking at each step if all tasks align (i.e., time is divisible by each task's cycle). Stop when the first sync time is found. This is straightforward but inefficient for large cycles.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(k * LCM(cycles)) where k is the number of tasks, but effectively high due to LCM growth

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Sync Time for Recurring Tasks Using Brute Force Iteration
ladders.desc: Given an array of task cycle times, find the earliest time when all tasks align simultaneously.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the smallest positive time T such that T is divisible by every task's cycle time. The brute force approach iterates through time steps starting from the maximum cycle time, checking divisibility for all tasks at each step. Intuitively, tasks align when their cycles complete simultaneously, which mathematically means T must be a common multiple of all cycle times. The earliest sync time is the least common multiple (LCM), but we find it through iteration rather than direct LCM calculation. The algorithm begins at max_cycle (since sync time must be at least this large) and increments time by 1, checking if time % cycle == 0 for all cycles. It stops at the first time satisfying all conditions. Time complexity is O(k * LCM(cycles)) in worst case, where k is number of tasks, because we might iterate up to the LCM. Space is O(1) as we only use variables. Connection to L1: This uses the divisibility check subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include starting iteration from 1 (inefficient), not handling large cycles (timeout), or misunderstanding that sync time is LCM. Real pattern: Initialize time = max(cycles); while not all divisible: time += 1; return time.

examples.trace: Cycles: [5,10]. Max cycle = 10. Start time=10: Check 10%5=0 ✓, 10%10=0 ✓. All divisible immediately, return 10.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check Divisibility for All Tasks at Given Time
ladders.desc: Given a time T and list of cycles, determine if T is divisible by every cycle.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core check in brute force iteration. Intuition: For tasks to sync at time T, each task must have completed an integer number of cycles, meaning T must be a multiple of each cycle time. The function iterates through cycles, checking if T % cycle == 0 for all. If any remainder is non-zero, return false; otherwise true. Step-by-step: Initialize; loop through cycles; if T % cycle != 0, return false; after loop, return true. Time: O(k) where k is cycles count, as we check each once. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This check is called repeatedly in the main loop. Common mistakes: Using division instead of modulo, not handling cycle=0 (invalid input), or early exit on first true instead of checking all. Real pattern: for cycle in cycles: if time % cycle != 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Check 6%2=0 ✓, 6%3=0 ✓. Both divisible, return true.
Check 7%2=1 ✗ (non-zero). Immediately return false without checking 7%3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Search for First Common Multiple
ladders.desc: Iterate through increasing time values to find the first time divisible by all given numbers.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique: instead of computing LCM directly, we search linearly through time. Intuition: The sync time is the least common multiple (LCM), but we find it by testing consecutive integers starting from a lower bound. We start at max(cycles) because the LCM must be at least as large as the largest cycle. Then increment time by 1, checking divisibility each step, stopping at first match. Why it works: By checking every integer, we guarantee finding the smallest common multiple. Step-by-step: Find max_cycle; set time = max_cycle; while true: if time divisible by all cycles (using L1 subroutine), return time; else time += 1. Time: O(LCM) in worst case, as we may iterate up to LCM. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: Uses divisibility check. Common mistakes: Starting from 1 (inefficient), not incrementing correctly, or infinite loop if no sync (but LCM always exists for positive integers). Real pattern: time = max(cycles); while not all_divisible(time, cycles): time += 1; return time.

examples.trace: Max cycle = 3. Start time=3: Check divisibility: 3%2=1 ✗. Increment to 4: 4%2=0 ✓, 4%3=1 ✗. 5: 5%2=1 ✗. 6: 6%2=0 ✓, 6%3=0 ✓. All divisible, return 6.

ladders.title: Building Block — Find Maximum Value in an Array
ladders.desc: Given an array of numbers, find the largest value.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is needed to determine the starting point for iteration. Intuition: The sync time must be at least as large as the longest task cycle, so we start searching from that value to avoid unnecessary checks on smaller times. The algorithm scans the array, keeping track of the maximum seen so far. Step-by-step: Initialize max_val to first element (or negative infinity if array empty, but problem guarantees non-empty). Loop through each element: if element > max_val, update max_val. After loop, return max_val. Time: O(n) where n is array length. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: Provides max_cycle for iterative search. Common mistakes: Not handling empty arrays, initializing max_val incorrectly (e.g., to 0 when numbers could be negative, but cycles are positive here), or using built-in functions without understanding. Real pattern: max_val = cycles[0]; for val in cycles[1:]: if val > max_val: max_val = val; return max_val.

examples.trace: Start max_val = 2. Check 5: 5 > 2, update max_val=5. Check 3: 3 > 5? No. Check 1: 1 > 5? No. Return 5.
Start max_val = 10. Check second 10: 10 > 10? No. Check third 10: 10 > 10? No. Return 10.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo (Remainder) Calculation
ladders.desc: Compute the remainder when one integer is divided by another.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation checks divisibility: a % b gives remainder when a is divided by b. Intuition: If remainder is 0, then a is divisible by b (i.e., a is a multiple of b). This is key to testing task alignment. Step-by-step: Given integers a and b (b > 0), perform integer division: quotient = a // b, remainder = a - (b * quotient). Alternatively, use built-in modulo operator. Time: O(1) typically. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: Used in divisibility check. Common mistakes: Confusing modulo with division, handling b=0 (division by zero, but cycles are positive), or misunderstanding negative remainders (not needed here as times and cycles are non-negative). Real pattern: remainder = a % b.

examples.trace: 10 divided by 3: quotient = 3, remainder = 10 - (3*3) = 10 - 9 = 1. So 10 % 3 = 1.
12 divided by 4: quotient = 3, remainder = 12 - (4*3) = 12 - 12 = 0. So 12 % 4 = 0, meaning 12 is divisible by 4.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Division and Multiples
ladders.desc: Understand that if a is divisible by b, then a is a multiple of b.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: the idea of multiples and divisibility. Intuition: A number a is a multiple of b if you can divide a by b with no remainder. For example, 6 is a multiple of 2 because 6/2=3 exactly. In task synchronization, if a task has cycle time b, it aligns at times that are multiples of b. Step-by-step: To check if a is a multiple of b, verify that a % b == 0. This means b divides a evenly. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L4: Forms basis for modulo operation. Common mistakes: Thinking multiples are only larger numbers (but 0 is a multiple of any number), or confusing with factors. Real pattern: is_multiple = (a % b == 0).

examples.trace: Check 8 % 2 = 0. Since remainder is 0, 8 is divisible by 2, so 8 is a multiple of 2. Yes, 2*4=8.
Check 7 % 3 = 1. Remainder is not 0, so 7 is not divisible by 3, meaning 7 is not a multiple of 3. Indeed, 3*2=6, 3*3=9, so 7 is between multiples.

approaches.name: Approach 2: LCM via GCD
approaches.summary: Compute the least common multiple (LCM) of all task cycles using the formula LCM(a,b) = (a*b)/GCD(a,b). Apply this iteratively across the list to find the overall sync time. This leverages number theory for efficiency.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(k * log(max(cycles))) where k is the number of tasks

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Sync Time for Recurring Tasks Using LCM via GCD
ladders.desc: Given a list of task cycle times, compute when all tasks will first synchronize using iterative LCM calculation.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires finding the earliest time when multiple recurring tasks with different cycle times all align. This sync time is mathematically equivalent to the least common multiple (LCM) of all cycle times. The LCM represents the smallest positive integer divisible by each cycle time. We compute LCM efficiently using the relationship LCM(a,b) = (a*b)/GCD(a,b), where GCD is the greatest common divisor. Starting with the first cycle time as the initial LCM, we iteratively update it by computing LCM(current_lcm, next_cycle) for each subsequent task. This approach leverages number theory to avoid brute-force checking of multiples. Intuition: Tasks synchronize at multiples of their cycles; the first common multiple across all tasks is the LCM. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize result = cycles[0]. 2. For each cycle in cycles[1:]: a. Compute gcd = GCD(result, cycle) using Euclidean algorithm. b. Update result = (result * cycle) // gcd. 3. Return result. Time: O(k * log(max(cycles))) due to k-1 GCD computations, each O(log(max(a,b))). Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This uses the iterative LCM function as the key subroutine. Common mistakes: Not using integer division (//) leading to floats, or handling empty input (should return 0 or handle per problem spec). Real code pattern: def sync_time(cycles): result = cycles[0]; for c in cycles[1:]: g = gcd(result, c); result = result * c // g; return result.

examples.trace: Start: cycles = [2,3,4]. Step 1: result = 2 (first cycle). Step 2: Process cycle=3: gcd(2,3)=1, result = (2*3)//1 = 6. Step 3: Process cycle=4: gcd(6,4)=2, result = (6*4)//2 = 24//2 = 12. Final result = 12. Verification: 12 is divisible by 2,3,4 (12/2=6, 12/3=4, 12/4=3).

Start: cycles = [5,10,15]. Step 1: result = 5. Step 2: Process cycle=10: gcd(5,10)=5, result = (5*10)//5 = 50//5 = 10. Step 3: Process cycle=15: gcd(10,15)=5, result = (10*15)//5 = 150//5 = 30. Final result = 30. Verification: 30/5=6, 30/10=3, 30/15=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative LCM of a List Using GCD
ladders.desc: Compute the LCM of multiple integers by iteratively applying LCM(a,b) = (a*b)/GCD(a,b).
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the full problem, computing the LCM for an entire list of numbers efficiently. The intuition is that LCM is associative: LCM(a,b,c) = LCM(LCM(a,b), c). Thus, we can start with the first number and repeatedly combine it with each subsequent number using the binary LCM operation. The binary LCM uses the formula LCM(a,b) = (a*b)/GCD(a,b), which avoids enumerating multiples. Step-by-step: 1. If list is empty, return 1 (or handle as per context). 2. Initialize lcm_val = first element. 3. For each num in rest of list: a. Compute g = GCD(lcm_val, num). b. Update lcm_val = (lcm_val * num) // g. 4. Return lcm_val. Time: O(n * log(max(a,b))) for n numbers, due to GCD computations. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This function directly implements the sync time calculation. Connection to L2: It relies on the binary LCM formula. Common mistakes: Forgetting integer division, not handling zero or negative numbers (assume positive inputs here). Real code pattern: def lcm_list(nums): if not nums: return 1; res = nums[0]; for n in nums[1:]: g = gcd(res, n); res = res * n // g; return res.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary LCM Formula: LCM(a,b) = (a*b)/GCD(a,b)
ladders.desc: Compute the least common multiple of two numbers using their greatest common divisor.
ladders.explanation: This is the central mathematical insight enabling efficient LCM computation. The intuition stems from prime factorization: LCM(a,b) includes all prime factors from both numbers at their highest powers, while GCD(a,b) includes common factors at their lowest powers. Multiplying a and b gives all factors twice for common ones, so dividing by GCD removes the duplication. Formally, a*b = LCM(a,b) * GCD(a,b). Step-by-step: 1. Compute g = GCD(a, b). 2. Compute lcm = (a * b) // g. Ensure integer division. Time: O(log(min(a,b))) dominated by GCD. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This formula is used in each iteration of the list LCM. Connection to L3: It builds on understanding of GCD. Common mistakes: Using float division or not handling large numbers (use integer ops). Real code pattern: def lcm(a, b): return a * b // gcd(a, b).

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute GCD(6,9). 9 % 6 = 3, 6 % 3 = 0, so GCD=3. Step 2: LCM = (6*9)//3 = 54//3 = 18. Verification: Multiples of 6: 6,12,18,...; of 9: 9,18,...; first common is 18.

ladders.title: Building Block — Euclidean Algorithm for GCD
ladders.desc: Compute the greatest common divisor of two numbers using the Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: GCD is essential for the LCM formula. The Euclidean algorithm is based on the property that GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b). Intuition: If d divides both a and b, it also divides their difference and remainder. By repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder, we reduce the problem until the remainder is zero, at which point the other number is the GCD. Step-by-step: 1. While b != 0: a, b = b, a % b. 2. Return a. This works for non-negative integers. Time: O(log(min(a,b))) as numbers reduce quickly. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: GCD is used in the LCM formula. Connection to L4: It relies on modulo operation. Common mistakes: Not handling zero correctly (GCD(a,0)=a), or infinite loops with negative numbers (use absolute values if needed). Real code pattern: def gcd(a, b): while b: a, b = b, a % b; return a.

examples.trace: Step 1: a=48, b=18. Compute 48 % 18 = 12. Update: a=18, b=12. Step 2: 18 % 12 = 6. Update: a=12, b=6. Step 3: 12 % 6 = 0. Update: a=6, b=0. Since b=0, return a=6. Verification: Divisors of 48: 1,2,3,4,6,8,12,16,24,48; of 18: 1,2,3,6,9,18; common: 1,2,3,6; greatest is 6.
Step 1: a=17, b=13. 17 % 13 = 4. Update: a=13, b=4. Step 2: 13 % 4 = 1. Update: a=4, b=1. Step 3: 4 % 1 = 0. Update: a=1, b=0. Return a=1. Verification: 17 and 13 are primes, so GCD=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo (Remainder) Operation
ladders.desc: Compute the remainder when one integer is divided by another.
ladders.explanation: The modulo operation, denoted a % b, returns the remainder of a divided by b. It is fundamental for the Euclidean algorithm. Intuition: When dividing a by b, we get quotient q and remainder r such that a = b*q + r, with 0 ≤ r < b. The modulo gives r. For example, 7 % 3 = 1 because 7 = 3*2 + 1. Step-by-step: To compute a % b, subtract b from a repeatedly until the result is less than b, or use built-in operators. Time: O(1) typically. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: Modulo is used in each step of the Euclidean algorithm to reduce numbers. Connection to L5: It builds on integer division. Common mistakes: Confusing with integer division (//), or misunderstanding negative remainders (in Python, a % b always returns non-negative r). Real code pattern: remainder = a % b.

examples.trace: Divide 10 by 3: quotient = 3, remainder = 10 - 3*3 = 10 - 9 = 1. So 10 % 3 = 1.
Divide 17 by 5: quotient = 3, remainder = 17 - 5*3 = 17 - 15 = 2. So 17 % 5 = 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Division and Multiples
ladders.desc: Understand division of integers and the concept of multiples.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves dividing integers to get a quotient and remainder, and understanding that a multiple of a number n is any integer of the form n*k for integer k. Intuition: Division splits a number into equal parts; multiples are the results of repeated addition. For example, multiples of 3 are 3,6,9,... Step-by-step: To divide a by b (b>0), find how many times b fits into a (quotient) and what's left (remainder). Multiples are generated by multiplying the number by 1,2,3,... Time: O(1) for basic ops. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: Integer division is related to modulo (remainder). Connection to higher levels: LCM involves finding common multiples. Common mistakes: Confusing with floating-point division, or not understanding that multiples include the number itself (e.g., 5 is a multiple of 5). Real code pattern: quotient = a // b; multiple = n * k.

examples.trace: 10 divided by 3: 3 fits into 10 three times (3*3=9), with 1 left over. So quotient = 3, remainder = 1.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Prime Factorization LCM
approaches.summary: Factor each task cycle into prime factors, then compute LCM by taking the highest power of each prime across all cycles. Multiply these to get the sync time. This method is useful when prime factors are needed for other computations.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(p) where p is the number of distinct primes

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Sync Time Using Prime Factorization LCM
ladders.desc: Given multiple task cycles, compute their sync time using prime factorization to find the LCM.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding when multiple recurring tasks with different cycle lengths will synchronize. The sync time is the least common multiple (LCM) of all cycle lengths. Using prime factorization provides an efficient method to compute LCM, especially when dealing with large numbers or when prime factors are needed for other computations. The intuition is that any number can be uniquely expressed as a product of prime powers. The LCM must contain each prime factor at the highest power it appears in any of the cycle lengths to ensure all cycles divide the sync time. The algorithm involves: 1) Factor each cycle length into prime factors with their exponents. 2) For each distinct prime, track the maximum exponent across all factorizations. 3) Multiply all primes raised to their maximum exponents to get the LCM. Time complexity is O(k * sqrt(max(cycles))) where k is the number of cycles, as each factorization takes O(sqrt(n)). Space complexity is O(p) for storing distinct primes and their max exponents. This connects to the parent problem by providing the complete solution. Common mistakes include not handling large numbers (use integer types), forgetting to take maximum exponents, or inefficient factorization. Real code pattern: def prime_factorization_lcm(cycles): prime_powers = {} for num in cycles: factors = factorize(num) for prime, exp in factors.items(): prime_powers[prime] = max(prime_powers.get(prime, 0), exp) lcm = 1 for prime, exp in prime_powers.items(): lcm *= prime ** exp return lcm

examples.trace: Step 1: Factor each cycle. 6 = 2^1 * 3^1, 15 = 3^1 * 5^1, 10 = 2^1 * 5^1. Step 2: Track max exponents: prime 2 max exp = 1, prime 3 max exp = 1, prime 5 max exp = 1. Step 3: LCM = 2^1 * 3^1 * 5^1 = 2 * 3 * 5 = 30.
Step 1: Factor each cycle. 12 = 2^2 * 3^1, 18 = 2^1 * 3^2, 24 = 2^3 * 3^1. Step 2: Track max exponents: prime 2 max exp = 3, prime 3 max exp = 2. Step 3: LCM = 2^3 * 3^2 = 8 * 9 = 72.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Prime Factorization of a Single Number
ladders.desc: Factor a given integer into its prime factors with exponents.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine decomposes a number into its prime factors, which is essential for computing LCM via prime factorization. The intuition is that every integer greater than 1 can be uniquely expressed as a product of prime numbers raised to powers (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). This factorization helps in comparing and combining factors across multiple numbers. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty dictionary to store prime factors and their exponents. 2) For each prime candidate starting from 2, while the number is divisible by the prime, divide the number by the prime and count the exponent. 3) Increment the prime candidate (optimization: after 2, only check odd numbers). 4) Stop when the number becomes 1 or the prime candidate squared exceeds the current number (if remaining number > 1, it's a prime). Time complexity is O(sqrt(n)) as we check divisors up to sqrt(n). Space complexity is O(log n) for storing factors. This feeds into L0 by providing factorized representations for each cycle. Common mistakes include not handling large primes efficiently, infinite loops, or incorrect exponent counting. Real code pattern: def factorize(n): factors = {} while n % 2 == 0: factors[2] = factors.get(2, 0) + 1; n //= 2. p = 3 while p * p <= n: while n % p == 0: factors[p] = factors.get(p, 0) + 1; n //= p. p += 2. if n > 1: factors[n] = factors.get(n, 0) + 1. return factors.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=60, divisible by 2: 60/2=30, exp=1; 30/2=15, exp=2. Step 2: n=15, not divisible by 2, move to 3: 15/3=5, exp=1. Step 3: n=5, not divisible by 3, move to 5: 5/5=1, exp=1. Step 4: n=1, stop. Factors: 2^2, 3^1, 5^1.
Step 1: n=17, not divisible by 2. Step 2: p=3, 3*3=9 <=17, 17%3!=0. p=5, 5*5=25>17, stop. Step 3: n=17>1, so 17 is prime. Factors: 17^1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compute LCM from Prime Factorizations
ladders.desc: Given prime factorizations of multiple numbers, compute their LCM by taking maximum exponents.
ladders.explanation: This core logic combines prime factorizations of multiple numbers to find their least common multiple. The intuition is that the LCM must be divisible by each number, meaning it must include every prime factor present in any number, raised to at least the highest power that appears for that prime across all numbers. This ensures minimality because using lower exponents would not cover all numbers. The algorithm: 1) Initialize a dictionary to track maximum exponents for each prime. 2) For each number's factorization (as a dictionary of prime: exponent), update the global dictionary by taking the max of current exponent and new exponent for each prime. 3) After processing all numbers, compute LCM by multiplying each prime raised to its maximum exponent. Time complexity is O(p * k) where p is number of distinct primes and k is number of numbers, but typically p is small. Space complexity is O(p) for storing max exponents. This connects to L1 by using factorized numbers as input. Common mistakes include not initializing exponents correctly, missing primes, or incorrect multiplication order. Real code pattern: def lcm_from_factors(factor_list): max_exps = {}. for factors in factor_list: for prime, exp in factors.items(): max_exps[prime] = max(max_exps.get(prime, 0), exp). lcm = 1. for prime, exp in max_exps.items(): lcm *= prime ** exp. return lcm.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize max_exps = {}. Step 2: Process first: prime 2 exp=1 -> max_exps[2]=1, prime 3 exp=1 -> max_exps[3]=1. Step 3: Process second: prime 3 exp=1 -> max_exps[3]=max(1,1)=1, prime 5 exp=1 -> max_exps[5]=1. Step 4: Process third: prime 2 exp=1 -> max_exps[2]=max(1,1)=1, prime 5 exp=1 -> max_exps[5]=max(1,1)=1. Step 5: LCM = 2^1 * 3^1 * 5^1 = 2*3*5=30.
Step 1: max_exps = {}. Step 2: Process first: 2->2, 3->1. Step 3: Process second: 2->max(2,1)=2, 3->max(1,2)=2. Step 4: Process third: 2->max(2,3)=3, 3->max(2,1)=2. Step 5: LCM = 2^3 * 3^2 = 8*9=72.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate and Update Maximum Values in a Dictionary
ladders.desc: Update a dictionary to store the maximum value for each key across multiple inputs.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves maintaining a dictionary where keys are items (like prime numbers) and values are numbers (like exponents), updating each key's value to the maximum encountered across multiple sources. The intuition is that in many algorithms, we need to aggregate information from multiple inputs by taking the extreme value (max or min) per category. This is efficient because dictionaries provide O(1) average access and update. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty dictionary. 2) For each input source (e.g., a dictionary of key-value pairs), iterate through its items. 3) For each key-value pair, check if the key exists in the global dictionary; if not, add it with the current value. If it exists, update the value to the maximum of the current value and the new value. 4) Continue until all sources are processed. Time complexity is O(n) where n is total number of key-value pairs across all sources. Space complexity is O(k) for storing distinct keys. This connects to L2 by providing the mechanism to track maximum exponents. Common mistakes include not handling missing keys correctly, using min instead of max, or modifying the dictionary while iterating. Real code pattern: def update_max_dict(global_dict, new_dict): for key, value in new_dict.items(): if key in global_dict: global_dict[key] = max(global_dict[key], value). else: global_dict[key] = value.

examples.trace: Step 1: global={}. Step 2: Process new: key='a' value=5 -> 'a' not in global, add global['a']=5. Step 3: key='b' value=3 -> 'b' not in global, add global['b']=3. Result: {'a':5, 'b':3}.

Step 1: global={'x':2, 'y':4}. Step 2: Process new: key='x' value=3 -> 'x' in global, max(2,3)=3, update global['x']=3. Step 3: key='y' value=1 -> 'y' in global, max(4,1)=4, no change. Step 4: key='z' value=5 -> 'z' not in global, add global['z']=5. Result: {'x':3, 'y':4, 'z':5}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Modulo for Factorization
ladders.desc: Use division and modulo operations to extract factors from a number.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves repeatedly dividing a number by a divisor to check for factors and reduce the number, using integer division (//) and modulo (%). The intuition is that if a number n is divisible by a divisor d (i.e., n % d == 0), then d is a factor, and we can divide n by d to reduce it and continue checking for more factors of d. This is fundamental in factorization algorithms. The algorithm: 1) While n % d == 0, perform n = n // d and count how many times this happens to get the exponent. 2) Use this in a loop to test multiple divisors. Time complexity depends on the number of divisions, but each division is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for variables. This connects to L3 by providing the low-level operations needed to build factorizations. Common mistakes include using floating-point division, not updating n correctly, or infinite loops when n becomes 0. Real code pattern: while n % divisor == 0: count += 1; n //= divisor.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=12, 12%2==0 -> true, count=1, n=12//2=6. Step 2: n=6, 6%2==0 -> true, count=2, n=6//2=3. Step 3: n=3, 3%2==0 -> false, stop. Result: count=2, n=3.
Step 1: n=17, 17%3==2 !=0 -> false, stop. Result: count=0, n=17.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand what prime numbers are and how divisibility works.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers prime numbers and the basic idea of divisibility, which is essential for factorization. A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. Examples: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11. Divisibility means that an integer a is divisible by an integer b (b ≠ 0) if there exists an integer k such that a = b * k. We say b divides a, written as b | a. The intuition is that primes are the building blocks of all numbers—any integer can be broken down into a unique product of primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). This is why factorization works: by repeatedly dividing by primes, we decompose a number. Understanding this helps in algorithms that involve factors, multiples, or LCM. Key points: 1 is not prime. 2 is the only even prime. Divisibility can be checked using the modulo operator (a % b == 0). This connects to L4 by providing the theoretical basis for why division and modulo are used in factorization. Common mistakes include thinking 1 is prime, or confusing divisibility with factors. Real code pattern: def is_prime(n): if n < 2: return False. for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if n % i == 0: return False. return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check if 12 is prime: 12 > 1, but 12 % 2 == 0, so divisible by 2 -> not prime. Step 2: Find divisors: 12 / 2 = 6, 12 / 3 = 4, 12 / 4 = 3, 12 / 6 = 2. So divisors: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12.
Step 1: Check if 7 is prime: 7 > 1. Step 2: Test divisors from 2 to sqrt(7)≈2.6: 7 % 2 == 1, not divisible. Step 3: No divisors found, so 7 is prime. Divisors: 1 and 7.
